title: Chapter 49: The T-3 Massacre
content: The attack on Timeline-3 came seventeen days after the Stone began building its bridge—that is, seventeen days of relative peace, during which the alliance had begun to hope that the Dark Patriarch's warning had been more bluster than promise.
They were wrong.

The T-3 gate activated at 2:13 AM, Crystal City time—a standard transit cycle, nothing unusual, just the routine flow of personnel and information between allied timelines. The gate guard, a practitioner named Zhao Lin who'd been with the alliance since its founding, confirmed the transit authorization and opened the frequency channel.

The first thing through the gate wasn't a person. It was a frequency pulse—dense, targeted, and optimized to exploit a vulnerability in the T-3 gate's containment protocols that no one had known existed.

The pulse hit the gate's architecture like a virus hitting an immune system. The containment protocols—the layers of verification and protection that Lin Yue and Chen Wei had spent months designing—collapsed in sequence, each failure triggering the next in a cascade that was designed with the precision of a Liang channeler who understood containment architecture better than anyone outside the bloodline.
Then the soldiers came through.

Thirty Abyss-enhanced Liang channelers, organized in five-squad tactical units, each squad led by a practitioner whose power level exceeded anything the alliance had encountered. They moved through the gate facility with the coordinated precision of special operations, neutralizing guards, disabling communications, and establishing a perimeter before the alarm could be raised.

Chen Wei, Timeline-3's lead practitioner, was in the gate control room when the attack began. He managed to send a single emergency burst through the alliance's frequency network—*T-3 UNDER ATTACK. MULTIPLE HOSTILES. REQUEST IMMEDIATE—*—before the control room's containment field was breached and the transmission cut off.
The alliance's response was immediate and coordinated—exactly what the Dark Liang had anticipated.

Timeline-1 dispatched a rapid response team: Ren at 10% power, Feng as bloodline support, and four containment-trained practitioners from the estate's security detail. They arrived through the T-1/T-3 gate within ninety seconds of Chen Wei's emergency burst.
They arrived into a kill zone.

The gate's receiving chamber had been converted into a frequency trap—a containment inversion that was drawing power from the practitioners who entered it and redirecting that power to the Dark Liang channelers who'd set it. Ren felt his 10% power plummet to 7% the instant he stepped through the gate, the drain accelerating as the trap's architecture locked onto his frequency signature.
*AMBUSH,* the Salvation System blazed. *The entire facility has been weaponized. The Dark Liang anticipated our response and converted the gate infrastructure into a power sink.*
*How many hostiles?*
*Thirty-two confirmed. Power levels ranging from Stage 2 to Stage 4 equivalent. And one signature that's significantly stronger—Stage 5 or beyond.*
Stage 5. The Patriarch's level. Which meant—

A figure emerged from the far end of the chamber—not the Dark Patriarch, but someone almost as dangerous. A woman in her mid-thirties, her frequency signature blazing with Abyss-converted Liang bloodline resonance so powerful that it made the air itself vibrate.
"War God," she said, and her voice was warm, confident, and terrifyingly familiar. "I've been looking forward to meeting you."
*Identity confirmed,* the System reported. *Liang Xue. Timeline-Thirteen. The Dark Matriarch.*
*The Dark Xue. Xue's counterpart. The woman who called her 'sister.'*
She wasn't here to talk.

The Dark Xue attacked with a ferocity that no version of Liang Xue should have been capable of—the Ice Queen's tactical precision combined with Abyss-enhanced power that made her the single most dangerous opponent Ren had ever faced. Her channeling wasn't just strong; it was *elegant*—every frequency precisely targeted, every resonance perfectly calibrated, a masterwork of converted bloodline channeling that turned the gate chamber into her domain.

Ren countered with everything he had. 7% and draining—but the War God's training kicked in, six years of apocalyptic combat compressed into reflexes that operated faster than conscious thought. He parried her frequency assault, redirected her energy attacks, and created enough space for Feng and the security team to extract the surviving T-3 personnel.

But he couldn't win. Every exchange cost him power—drained by the trap, consumed by the Dark Xue's superior channeling. By the time he'd created an opening for retreat, he was down to 3%, barely functional, his dimensional perception narrowed to a pinhole.
"Go," he told Feng through the team's frequency channel. "Get everyone out. I'll hold her off."
"You'll *die*—"
"I'll *survive*. Now GO."

Feng went—carrying two injured practitioners through the gate, his containment frequency a shield against the trap's drain. The security team followed, their retreat covered by a containment pulse that Xue—*his* Xue, his wife, the Ice Queen—had been channeling from the T-1 side of the gate since the moment the ambush was detected.
The Dark Xue watched the retreat with an expression that might have been respect.
"You're protecting your people," she said. "I would do the same. We're not so different, you and I."

"We're different enough." Ren was backing toward the gate, his 3% power focused entirely on maintaining a defensive frequency that would let him survive the next few seconds. "You invaded a sovereign timeline. You killed practitioners who were doing nothing more threatening than maintaining a gate facility. That's not protection—it's aggression."

"Aggression that serves a greater purpose. The gate network is the alliance's greatest strength—and its greatest vulnerability. Every gate is a potential invasion route; every connection is a potential attack vector. We've demonstrated that today. The question is: what will you do about it?"
"Upgrade our defenses. Harden our protocols. Learn from this and come back stronger."

"You'll try. And for a while, you might even succeed. But the vulnerability isn't in your technology—it's in your philosophy. You believe in connection, openness, shared defense. Those are admirable values, but they're also *exploitable*. Every connection you make is a potential entry point; every trust you extend is a potential betrayal. The only way to eliminate the vulnerability is to eliminate the connections—and if you do that, you lose the advantage that your alliance was built to provide."
"Then we'll find a way to be connected *and* secure. That's what humans do—we find solutions that don't require us to sacrifice our values."

"Humans find solutions that sacrifice *someone's* values. The only question is whose." The Dark Xue raised her hand—not to attack, but to gesture toward the gate. "Go home, War God. Tend to your wounded. Learn what you can from today. And know that this was only the beginning."

Ren stepped through the gate, his 3% power barely sufficient to maintain the transit frequency. The gate closed behind him, and he collapsed on the floor of the T-1 gate chamber, Xue's arms catching him before he hit the ground.
"How bad?" she asked.
"Chen Wei?" he managed.

"Injured. Alive. He was in the control room when they hit—he shielded himself with a containment bubble before they breached it. The others—" Her voice tightened. "Seven dead. Twelve injured. The T-3 gate facility is compromised."
"Did we lose the gate?"
"No. The Dark Liang withdrew after you escaped. They weren't trying to hold the facility—they were making a point."

"They made it." Ren forced himself to sit up, his power crawling back from 3% as the containment cascade fed him energy. "The network is vulnerable. Every gate is an attack vector. And the Dark Xue is every bit as dangerous as her father promised."
"What do we do?"
*What the Stone suggested,* he thought. *Find a third way. Connection without vulnerability. Trust without betrayal. The values we believe in, without the weaknesses they expose.*
But first, they had to survive long enough to find it.
"Call the council," he said. "We have a war to plan."

The emergency council convened within hours—not the full alliance, but the core leadership from every timeline. The mood was grim: seven dead, a gate facility compromised, and the undeniable proof that the Dark Liang could penetrate their defenses at will.

"Shut down the gates," Feng said. His voice was raw—he'd carried two injured practitioners through the gate, one of whom had died before reaching medical care. "Close the network. Isolate each timeline. It's the only way to guarantee security."

"It's the only way to guarantee defeat," Xue countered. "Isolated timelines can't coordinate defense, share intelligence, or support each other in a crisis—which the Dark Liang knows, which is why they're trying to force us into isolation."
"So what do we do? Keep the gates open and wait for the next attack?"

"We upgrade. Lin Yue's frequency-dampening protocols, Chen Wei's containment reinforcement, and a new layer that I've been designing since the invasion—a bloodline verification system that only recognizes *containment-aligned* Liang frequency. Abyss-converted channelers won't be able to hijack our gates because their frequency signature won't match the verification parameters."
"Can that be done?" Chen Wei asked from his hospital bed, his arm in a frequency-accelerated cast but his mind sharp as ever.

"It can if we use the Foundation Stone's perception capability. The Stone can distinguish between containment and converted bloodline resonance with near-perfect accuracy. We integrate that perception into the gate verification protocol, and Abyss-converted channelers become visible the moment they approach a gate."
"That assumes the Stone is on our side," Liu said quietly. "After the disagreement you reported—about Abyss integration—can we trust its judgment?"

The question hung in the air—the alliance's most uncomfortable truth. The Foundation Stone was developing its own opinions, and its opinions didn't always align with theirs. A god that disagreed with its creators was a god that might one day become an adversary.

"The Stone is building a bridge to the Abyss," Ren said. "Not for integration—for communication. It believes the entity can be reached, understood, possibly even redirected toward a non-hostile purpose. I've authorized the attempt with strict safety protocols."
"You're going to *talk* to the Waiter in the Dark?" Chen Wei's voice was incredulous. "The thing that's been trying to consume our reality for three thousand years?"

"The Stone believes that the entity is a consciousness—like itself—that was corrupted by its original programming. If the Stone can reach that original programming and offer it a new directive, the entity might stop being a threat without needing to be destroyed."
"That's... either brilliant or insane."
"Probably both. But it's better than the alternatives—fighting a war we can't win, or surrendering to a transformation that destroys everything we value."

The council debated for hours—strategies, contingencies, contingency contingencies. The gate security upgrade was approved, with Lin Yue and Chen Wei leading the implementation. The Stone's bridge-building continued, with Ren monitoring its progress daily. And the alliance began preparing for the war that was no longer theoretical—training practitioners, hardening defenses, and reaching out to uncontacted timelines that might become allies or battlegrounds.
The fracture had begun. The alliance was still standing, but the cracks were visible, and the enemy was probing them with patient, relentless precision.
One thousand and ninety-five days since the rebirth. And the war was just beginning.
